name: Cockpit
classification_notes: Cockpit is a condensed reverse-contrast typeface with mechanical, utilitarian forms derived from airport runway lettering. The reverse-contrast construction and perspective-scaling concept place it firmly in the display category rather than any conventional text classification.
summary: A condensed reverse-contrast display typeface in four weights with obliques, drawn from the distorted lettering painted on airport runways and designed to account for cockpit viewing angles.
description: The concept behind Cockpit is perspective scaling: runway markings are elongated to appear proportional when viewed from a steep angle above. That functional logic drives the entire design, producing mechanically constructed letterforms with intentional imperfection and no decorative concession. Four weights from Regular to Black each come with an oblique, giving eight styles in total.
foundry_blurb: Feliciano Type describe Cockpit as a condensed reverse-contrast typeface inspired by the stretched lettering found on airport runways, where letters are scaled to compensate for the viewing angle from the cockpit. Its mechanical forms and systematic construction embrace simple, unrefined shapes that prioritise function over embellishment, delivering a powerful and distinct visual impact across four weights with obliques.

name: Acceptera
classification_notes: A contemporary sans-serif rooted in Swedish functionalist modernism, with clean practical forms suited to text use; the humanist sans subcategory fits its legibility-first approach and organic origins in early 20th-century modernist design.
summary: A sans-serif typeface drawing on Swedish functionalism, named after the 1930 modernist manifesto by Gunnar Asplund and his contemporaries.
description: Available in seven weights from Light to Black with matching italics, the family is designed for text use with a clarity that reflects the functionalist ethos of early 20th-century Swedish architecture. The name references Acceptera!, the manifesto that advocated functionalism in architecture and design, and the letterforms follow that same logic of clean, practical construction. Fourteen styles in total at a single width, suited to editorial, wayfinding and corporate work where restraint is preferred.
foundry_blurb: Feliciano Type describe Acceptera as a contemporary sans-serif balancing functionality and restraint, inspired by Swedish functionalism and the modernist principles of the 1930 Stockholm Exhibition. The name pays homage to the influential Acceptera! manifesto by architects Gunnar Asplund, Wolter Gahn, Sven Markelius, Eskil Sundahl, Uno Åhrén and art historian Gregor Paulsson, whose advocacy for functionalism in architecture and design is echoed in the typeface's clean, practical forms.

name: Correct Condensed
classification_notes: A flat-sided condensed grotesque sans inspired by mid-century commercial typefaces such as Compacta and Metropol; its tight proportions, even width variants and mechanical-yet-human character place it firmly in the condensed grotesque tradition.
summary: A flat-sided condensed grotesque in five widths and six weights, drawing on the proportional calm of Aldo Novarese's overlooked Metropol to update the bold commercial condensed style of the 1960s.
description: Rooted in car racing posters and bold magazine layouts of the late 1960s, it takes particular cues from Metropol's even proportions and gentle curves rather than the more celebrated Compacta or Helvetica Compressed. One weight axis runs across five widths from Condensed to XXXX Condensed, each with matching italics, giving twelve styles per sub-family. The result sits between display work and strong editorial moments without tipping into pastiche.
foundry_blurb: Feliciano Type describe Correct as coming from a graphic designer's love of car racing posters and bold magazine layouts from the late 1960s. The design blends Metropol's underappreciated qualities — even proportions, gentle curves and a subtle mechanical-human harmony — with a modern, functional perspective. It is available in one weight over five widths with matching italics, intended not as a daily workhorse but as a typeface with a strong yet understated personality that excels in the right setting.

name: Crisol
classification_notes: A stencil display typeface with geometric construction inspired by Futura Black and Braggadocio, with triangular terminals and six width variants designed for large-scale editorial use.
summary: A geometric stencil display typeface in six widths, distinguished from Futura Black and Braggadocio by triangular rather than circular terminals.
description: Originally released in 2017, Crisol received a revised Poster version in 2023 that shares the geometric principles of Futura Black and Braggadocio while replacing circular terminals with triangular ones for greater formal consistency across the family. Six width variants run from Wide to XXX Condensed, giving the family unusual range for a stencil face. Diacritics are drawn to match the thinner strokes rather than the overall weight, which aids tight-leaded all-caps setting and produces dense, texturally active typographic blocks.
foundry_blurb: Feliciano Type describe Crisol Poster (2023) as a revised version of Crisol (2017), a stencil display typeface available in six widths and inspired by the design principles of Futura Black by Paul Renner and Braggadocio by William A. Woolley. They note that its triangular terminals, rather than circular ones, give the typeface greater shape consistency than its predecessors, and that diacritics designed to match the thinner strokes allow lively, texturally rich typographic blocks when set in capitals with tight leading.

name: Eudald News
classification_notes: Inspired by the 18th-century Spanish punchcutter Eudald Pradell, who showed influences from Fleischmann, Rosart, Fournier and Bodoni, placing it squarely in transitional serif territory with bracket serifs and moderate contrast.
summary: A text typeface rooted in the 18th-century Spanish punchcutter Eudald Pradell, distilled into four weights with italics and small caps for contemporary use.
description: Drawing on the work of Eudald Pradell (1721–1788), often credited as the first to successfully cut type punches in Spain, this revival absorbs Fleischmann and Rosart influences alongside the elegance of Fournier and Bodoni. Four weights run from Regular to Bold, each with matching italics, and the family includes extended character sets with small caps for both roman and italic styles. The result is a historically grounded text face suited to editorial and book work where warmth and classical proportion matter.
foundry_blurb: Feliciano Type describe Eudald as a text typeface inspired by the 18th-century Spanish punchcutter Eudald Pradell, born in Ripoll, Catalonia, who learned punchcutting after training as an armourer. Pradell's typefaces show influences of Fleischmann and Rosart alongside the elegance of Fournier and Bodoni, and over several years Feliciano distilled those classical forms into a contemporary design with four weights, matching italics and small caps for both roman and italic.

name: Flama
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric sans serif drawing from both European and American traditions, with a clean, structured character suited to signage and editorial use.
summary: A geometric sans with roots in both European and American traditions, used widely in broadcast, newspaper and magazine publishing since 2004.
description: Designed originally for signage, Flama moved quickly into editorial environments and now appears in publications such as Politiken, The Sunday Times and Newsweek, as well as television networks. The family spans 50 styles across four width variants — Normal, Semi-Condensed, Condensed and Ultra-Condensed — each with ten weights from Thin to Black, and the Normal subfamily adds corresponding italics. The breadth of the collection makes it well suited to complex editorial systems where typographic hierarchy needs to work across print and screen.
foundry_blurb: Feliciano Type describe Flama as a geometric sans serif drawing inspiration from both European and American sans serif traditions. Originally designed for signage, it became a favourite with editorial designers and is widely used across television networks and international publications including Politiken, The Sunday Times and Newsweek. The complete collection comprises 50 styles across Normal, Semi-Condensed, Condensed and Ultra-Condensed families, each running from Thin to Black, with italics available for the Normal family.

name: Grosa
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a modernist sans serif with open forms synthesised from iconic historical sans serifs, placing it in a broad modernist sans tradition rather than a specific grotesque or geometric lineage.
summary: A modernist sans serif with open forms, available in six weights with matching italics and a companion monospaced variant.
description: Originally designed as the typeface for the Feliciano Type website, Grosa draws from a range of canonical sans serifs without directly reviving any single one. Six weights from Light to Black come with matching italics, giving 12 styles in the main family, while Grosa Mono adds a further six weights for code and tabular contexts. The open forms and careful construction make it serviceable across branding, signage, web and editorial work.
foundry_blurb: Feliciano Type describe Grosa as a modernist sans serif built on simple, carefully crafted open forms, positioning it as a powerful workhorse suitable for branding, signage, web and editorial design. Rather than reviving a pre-existing design, they characterise it as the common thread running through some of the most iconic sans serifs in history, released in 2020 across 12 styles.

name: Garda Nova
classification_notes: A capital-only display family derived from Renaissance calligraphy and Capitalis Monumentalis letterforms, spanning from high-contrast inscriptional serif to low-contrast humanist sans across three numbered variants.
summary: A capital-only display family in three variants, ranging from high-contrast inscriptional serifs faithful to Francesco Cresci's Renaissance calligraphy to a low-contrast humanist sans.
description: Rooted in the writings of Renaissance calligrapher Francesco Cresci and the Capitalis Monumentalis tradition, Garda Nova spans three numbered variants sharing the same underlying proportions and construction. No.1 (now revised and renamed Garda Titling) adheres closely to Cresci's original drawings with traditional inscriptional serifs; No.3 strips the contrast for a sans with a humanistic British character; No.2 occupies the middle ground. Ten styles across the collection have found use in architectural lettering, luxury packaging and refined stationery.
foundry_blurb: Feliciano Type describe Garda Nova as inspired by the writings of Renaissance master calligrapher Francesco Cresci, coming in three variants sharing the same proportion and construction based on traditional roman Capitalis Monumentalis letterforms. The 2023 revision of No.1, now called Garda Titling, adds a weight and improves the character set. Applications have ranged from architectural lettering to luxury packaging and stationery.

name: Grotzec
classification_notes: Rooted in late 19th-century European grotesque poster types, Grotzec is a condensed grotesque with subtle humanistic curve details that distinguish it from strictly mechanical grotesque models.
summary: A condensed grotesque drawn from late 19th-century European poster types, originally designed for Surf Portugal magazine where Feliciano served as art director.
description: Developed from close study of historical European grotesques found in late 19th-century posters and printed ephemera, the family introduces subtle humanistic inflections through varied curve speeds on the interior and exterior of letterforms. The core Grotzec Condensed family spans eight weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, while two additional sub-families extend the range: Grotzec X Condensed in three extreme-condensed black styles and Grotzec Poster in four widths optimised for very large sizes. The collection totals 23 styles across the full range.
foundry_blurb: Feliciano Type note that Grotzec Condensed was originally designed for Surf Portugal magazine during Mario Feliciano's tenure as art director, taking inspiration from early European grotesques in late 19th-century posters and advertisements. They describe how varied curve speeds on the exterior and interior of letterforms lend the design a subtle humanistic quality that sets it apart from a typical grotesque.

name: Mazagan
classification_notes: Inspired by Marocaines, an early 20th-century French serif display typeface, Mazagan is a wide-cut, high-contrast display serif developed for use at larger sizes across exhibition and editorial contexts.
summary: A wide-cut display serif revived from Marocaines, a French early 20th-century type specimen, expanded into eight styles from Medium to Super.
description: Based on Marocaines, an unusual display serif from the French foundry Mayeur originally conceived as a bold companion for Elzevir romans, Mazagan extends that source into a proper working family. Four weights from Medium to Super each have matching italics, giving the family enough range for book jackets, exhibition identities and editorial headlines. The wide proportions and sharp details give it a distinctive personality without resorting to novelty.
foundry_blurb: Feliciano Type describe Mazagan as inspired by Marocaines, an early 20th-century French display serif from the Mayeur foundry, where it was sold as a darker companion for Roman types lacking a bold weight, particularly the Elzevirs. They developed it into a full family of eight styles, noting that its wide cut, sharp details and unique shapes create a personable identity suited to exhibition work, book jackets and beyond.

name: Merlo Original
classification_notes: A revival of eighteenth-century typefaces distributed by the Madrid type founder Domingo Merlo, placing it within the transitional serif tradition of that period, with acknowledged French influence in its forms.
summary: A contemporary revival of eighteenth-century typefaces distributed by Madrid type founder Domingo Merlo, offered in four weights with matching italics for text composition.
description: Rooted in the Spanish typographic tradition of the latter eighteenth century, Merlo Original draws on types sold by Domingo Merlo in Madrid, though their punches were likely cut elsewhere, giving the design a recognisably French character. The family runs to eight styles across four weights from Regular to Bold, each with a matching italic, and features an extended glyph set optimised for continuous text. A first version of the typeface was reviewed by Matthew Carter as far back as 1998; this 2025 release represents a thorough reinterpretation of that earlier work.
foundry_blurb: Feliciano Type describe Merlo Original as a contemporary reinterpretation of eighteenth-century typefaces distributed by Domingo Merlo in Madrid. Unlike the Spanish punch-cutters of the period, Merlo was a type founder whose punches were likely produced elsewhere, lending the design a French character noted by Matthew Carter on reviewing an early version in 1998. The typeface is released in 2025 as eight styles across four weights with matching italics, with an extended glyph set carefully optimised for text composition.

name: Marcin Antique
classification_notes: Inspired by late 19th-century French grotesques from the Fonderie Gustave Mayeur, Marcin Antique sits between vintage grotesque and neo-grotesque, with only slight stroke modulation distinguishing it from both camps.
summary: A French grotesque revival drawn from 19th-century Fonderie Gustave Mayeur types, sitting between polished neo-grotesque and period-faithful reproduction.
description: Rooted in late 19th-century French type from the Fonderie Gustave Mayeur, this sans-serif avoids both the clinical finish of contemporary neo-grotesques and the self-conscious quirkiness of strict revivals. Only a minute modulation of stroke contrast separates it from its flattest peers. The 2022 version 2.0 expanded the family to 49 styles across four widths, from a standard cut with 11 weights and italics down to Condensed and X Condensed versions with 8 weights each.
foundry_blurb: Feliciano Type describe Marcin Antique as a fresh interpretation of France's long tradition of grotesque types, inspired by late 19th-century faces from the Fonderie Gustave Mayeur in Paris. They note it occupies a middle ground: less polished than most neo-grotesques, yet not over-emphasising the quirks of its vintage source material, with only a minute modulation in contrast. Version 2.0, released June 2022, added new widths, additional weights and completely redrawn italics, bringing the collection to 49 styles.

name: Miletus Grotesk
classification_notes: Explicitly inspired by early twentieth-century expressive grotesques and Swiss modernist structural precision, placing it firmly in the grotesque tradition of sans-serif design.
summary: A contemporary grotesque in seven weights with matching italics, drawing on early twentieth-century expressive models and Swiss modernist structure.
description: Seven weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic, give the family fourteen styles in total. The design blends the disciplined geometry of Swiss modernism with the warmth associated with American grotesques, kept in check by a restrained alternate character set. Positioned for performance across print and digital contexts, it aims for functional reliability without sacrificing typographic personality.
foundry_blurb: Feliciano Type describe Miletus Grotesk as inspired by the expressive grotesques of the early twentieth century, uniting Swiss modernist structural precision with the confident warmth of American design. Developed through a systematic design process, it offers a clear, distinctive voice with a restrained set of alternates, crafted for performance across print and digital media in seven finely tuned weights with matching italics.

name: Parafina
classification_notes: Parafina is a display typeface built on geometric shapes of varied widths, inspired by a mid-20th century hand-lettered alphabet. Its optical sizes and geometric construction place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A geometric display family in six weights with three optical sizes each, inspired by a mid-20th century hand-lettered alphabet by Spanish letterer Miguel Pedraza.
description: Designed by Feliciano Type and released in 2021, Parafina draws on a hand-lettered display alphabet by the Spanish letterer Miguel Pedraza without being a direct revival. The catalyst was Pedraza's work prompting a reconsideration of how a typographic system built on geometric shapes of varied widths could function. Each of the six weights — Thin through Black — comes with three optical sizes, so purchasing a single weight yields all three size-specific cuts.
foundry_blurb: Feliciano Type describe Parafina as inspired by a mid-20th century hand-lettered display alphabet by Spanish letterer Miguel Pedraza, though it is neither a translation of those shapes nor a revival of his work. That alphabet instead acted as a catalyst for rethinking a working typographic system based on geometric shapes of varied widths. The family is available in six weights, each including three optical sizes, with a single weight purchase granting access to all three optical cuts.

name: Parnaso
classification_notes: Parnaso is rooted in late 19th-century revivals of Old Style Roman types, specifically the Elzévir tradition as shown in Fonderie Gustave Mayeur specimens, placing it firmly in the humanist old-style serif category with baroque italic influences.
summary: A five-optical-size old-style revival rooted in late 19th-century Elzévir specimens, with vivid baroque italics and swash titling capitals suited to editorial and advertising work.
description: Drawing directly from the Fonderie Gustave Mayeur's 1894 and 1912 Elzévir specimen albums, the family translates that first wave of Old Style revivals into a contemporary magazine and advertising tool. Capital letters retain the geometric spirit of Didot but replace hairline serifs with sturdy wedge forms, while the condensed lowercase offers a distinctive type colour. Sixty styles span five optical sizes from Petit to Hairline, each in six weights with matching italics, plus a monospaced companion in Regular and Italic.
foundry_blurb: Feliciano Type describe Parnaso as a contemporary update of the first generation of Old Style Roman revivals from the late 19th century, when foundries pushed back against the dominance of Didot and Bodoni by releasing Antiqua and Elzévir types. The design was developed from the Fonderie Gustave Mayeur's Série XVIIe Siècle specimens, with the italic drawing on classical French baroque models and featuring swash titling capitals. The collection spans five optical sizes with six weights each, plus a mono companion.

name: Rongel
classification_notes: Inspired by 18th-century types from Spanish punch cutter Francisco Rongel, placing it firmly in the transitional serif tradition of Baroque-era Iberian typography.
summary: A text serif drawn from the 18th-century types of Spanish punch cutter Francisco Rongel, part of Feliciano Type's Iberian historical series.
description: Based on types documented in the 1799 Madrid specimen book of the Imprenta Real, Rongel revives the work of Francisco Rongel in three weights from Regular to Bold with matching italics. Its well-defined letterforms make it equally at home in running text and display settings. The family sits alongside Eudald News, Merlo and Geronimo as part of Feliciano Type's ongoing series of Iberian historical revivals.
foundry_blurb: Feliciano Type describe Rongel as a typeface inspired by 18th-century types from Spanish punch cutter Francisco Rongel, as showcased in Muestras de los punzones y matrices de letra, published in Madrid in 1799. Primarily a text face, its demarked shapes also suit display use. It is part of the foundry's Iberian historical series, alongside Eudald News, Merlo and Geronimo.

name: Statius
classification_notes: Statius is a reinterpretation of Berthold Wolpe's Albertus, a glyphic or lapidary serif with incised, chisel-cut letterforms rather than conventional calligraphic stress. The flared terminals and carved quality place it firmly in the glyphic category.
summary: A reinterpretation of Berthold Wolpe's Albertus begun from memory, departing from its most iconic letterforms to arrive at something both familiar and independent.
description: Designed at Feliciano Type and released in 2025, Statius takes Wolpe's Albertus as its starting point but deliberately abandons some of its most recognisable features, including the lowercase a and the uppercase M and R. The italic was drawn independently of the roman and includes a small set of swash capitals inspired by Wolpe's own work. Six weights with matching italics and a large character set make up the 12-style family.
foundry_blurb: Feliciano Type describe Statius as an obvious reinterpretation of Albertus through a contemporary and fresh approach, but note that in many senses it is also very different. It began as an attempt to design something like Albertus from memory, leading to deliberate departures in key letterforms, contrast and proportions, with an independently drawn italic that includes swash capitals inspired by Wolpe's work.

name: Sebenta
classification_notes: Sebenta is explicitly described as a slab serif in the Clarendon tradition, with low contrast, vertical stress and strong bracketed serifs, though it introduces old-style formal elements rather than a strictly modern type structure.
summary: A nine-weight slab serif drawing on Clarendon conventions while introducing old-style terminals, inspired by a 1938 Portuguese type specimen.
description: Sebenta takes the low-contrast, bracketed-serif structure of the Clarendon tradition and tempers it with angled terminals borrowed from old-style typefaces like Bookman, applied to both capitals and lowercase. The result softens the industrial solidity typical of most slab serifs with gentle modulations and ball terminals, giving the family an unusual warmth for the genre. Eighteen styles across nine weights with matching italics make it a credible workhorse for editorial, print and screen contexts.
foundry_blurb: Feliciano Type describe Sebenta as a contemporary slab serif inspired by an early twentieth-century monospaced typeface from the 1938 specimen of Fundição Tipográfica Gini. Rather than following the conventional modern-type structure of most Clarendons, Sebenta borrows angled and ball terminals from old-style typefaces, blending the modular character of the slab genre with softer modulations. The family's wide weight range and extended character set make it suitable for graphic, print or screen use.
